item.title: Thank You to Corning Life Sciences
item.description: Corning Life Sciences recently donated to the Molecular Sciences Institute's education training program. These lab consumable supplies are meant to provide expanded experimental teaching aids for students with the hope of increasing their passion for innovation.   “This teams well with MSI as part of their mission for the training course they offer to support intense, hands-on training courses for new scientists to find their passion”.    Corning's donation demonstrates their commitment,
item.dc:creator: David Kiewich
item.content:encoded:
Corning Life Sciences recently donated to the Molecular Sciences Institute's education training program. These lab consumable supplies are meant to provide expanded experimental teaching aids for students with the hope of increasing their passion for innovation.
“This teams well with MSI as part of their mission for the training course they offer to support intense, hands-on training courses for new scientists to find their passion”.
Corning's donation demonstrates their commitment, encapsulated in a campaign called "Corning Cares for the Future."  Corporate social responsibility is an essential part of the company and today's donation is an example of this dedication.
We are incredibly grateful for this generous donation of materials from Corning. These supplies will allow incoming students and trainees the freedom and materials they need to perform necessary experimental tasks in their pursuit of knowledge and their own passion for making a difference in the world. Thank you, Corning, for helping us fuel the innovators of tomorrow!
The Corning Family of BrandsCorning I PYREX I Axygen l Gosselin I FALCON
To learn more please visit the website link below:

item.title: Applications to the Biotech Training Program now open!
item.description: Great News!  The summer training course has been finalized.  There will be two sessions, each 5 weeks long.  I have included the video syllabus below for more information.  You can apply at our site.  We will only accept a maximum of 10 students per session, so apply soon to lock in your slot.     Remember, this will be a very intensive, hands-on training course; you will be in the lab nearly every day designing and executing experiments.   We have secured a large number of guest speakers for
item.dc:creator: David Kiewlich
item.content:encoded:
Great News!  The summer training course has been finalized.  There will be two sessions, each 5 weeks long.  I have included the video syllabus below for more information.  You can apply at our site.  We will only accept a maximum of 10 students per session, so apply soon to lock in your slot.
Remember, this will be a very intensive, hands-on training course; you will be in the lab nearly every day designing and executing experiments.
We have secured a large number of guest speakers for this course, including a major professor at Stanford University, a major professor at San Jose State, a visiting professor from Sweden and an Astronaut who will talk about biology in space and how lessons learned there are being used to help people on Earth.  We are signing up additional speakers continually.  Look forward to an updated list on our site.
I am looking forward to meeting you all this summer!

item.title: Finally, a team!
item.description: Last time I began to describe what makes a team and promised to finish.  There are 3 additional requirements to make a successful team, but I want to review briefly the first two.   The first requirement for the most successful team is picking the best people.  It goes without saying that mediocre talent does not create all-star results.  Don’t be afraid to pick your best people, unless you know that one or more are so unprofessional that they will be disruptive.  Even if this group hasn’t
item.dc:creator: Dr. David Kiewlich
item.content:encoded:
Last time I began to describe what makes a team and promised to finish.  There are 3 additional requirements to make a successful team, but I want to review briefly the first two.
The first requirement for the most successful team is picking the best people.  It goes without saying that mediocre talent does not create all-star results.  Don’t be afraid to pick your best people, unless you know that one or more are so unprofessional that they will be disruptive.  Even if this group hasn’t worked together before, I will explain how to get their buy in shortly.
The second requirement is to clearly define the team’s goal or series of goals if there is a logical progression.  No matter how talented the members, without a clear purpose, the team will lack focus.  Effort will either wither and fade, or worse yet, act at cross purposes.  DON’T assume that the members of the team will be able to “figure it out”.  Make it clear, up front, what this team was formed to do and make sure everyone clearly understands the goal.
Now that we have reviewed the first two requirements, let’s explore the final three.
3.Clearly define the shared success benefit for EACH member of the team.
Everyone is a bit mercenary when it comes to job performance, your all-stars are likely even more so.  They don’t work hard to make their boss’ bonus larger; they work hard for their own reward.  I am not suggesting that you should promote the “lone wolf” persona of win at all costs, but I want you to understand thata well-defined reward is motivating.
However, to encourage the team to work together, make it clear that the reward is dependent on the success of the entire team.Each member should know what they will get if the team succeeds.  This will bring the members together, since each individual’s reward will be dependent on the other members’ success.  Even people who don’t typically work well together will figure out a way, if they know their personal success is meaningless if the team fails.
4.Each member must make a sacrifice to be part of the team.
This frequently draws the most questions.  Why make people give something up to be part of the team?
Quite simply, nothing makes people value something more than having to give up something to get it.
For example:  Let’s say I give you a nice watch, I just hand it to you.  What value would you place on it?  Since it didn’t cost you anything, maybe you would wear it, but if it got lost or damaged, you might not give it a second thought.
Now, let’s say you bought that watch for $100.  You would be more likely to take care of it and protect it.  Let’s say you bought it for $5000 instead.  Now you are likely to take really good care of it, but more so, you are likely to want to show it off.External value helps individuals assign internal value.
Make joining the team dependent on some type of sacrifice.Initially it will inspire comradery of shared pain that each member will have in common, but also, as the team progresses toward its goal, the individual successes will be seen as shared accomplishments rather than individual triumphs.  Again, the motivation needs to be how to make the team stronger, not acknowledge the individual.
Pick a price to join, and make sure everyone in the team knows that everyone has paid that price.
So, if you have followed my suggestions, your team is made up of your best people, with clear goals, a clear reward for success and the bonding of a shared initial sacrifice.  What is left?  That would be the final requirement:
5.Establish a clear time period for the project.
Projects don’t last forever.  It isn’t a goal if the time frame is open ended, it is a wish.  Let me give you an example:  Which sounds more likely to get done, you have to increase sales 20% in one year or you have to increase sales 20% eventually?  Without a time boundary, your team won’t have a clear purpose and lack motivation to accomplish anything.
This might sound obvious, particularly from my trivial example, butit is easier to eliminate the time boundary than you might expect.
Does this sound familiar:a project is going well, then hits a snag and the team asks for more time to finish.  This request is granted, since they were making great progress, then something else comes up and the completion is delayed again.  Now there has been so much investment of time and money that something has to come from that investment, so the project continues.  On and on this continues until the project is wildly over budget and hugely delayed.
If failure is an option, then the team will know that they will lose something if they don’t succeed.  That is why there has to be shared sacrifice to be part of the team.By making the team members “ante up” at the beginning, they will know what they will lose by failing.Make it clear at the beginning if and how an extension of time will be granted and stick to those criteria.
Additionally, if a team fails, you and the members will learn something from the experience.Perhaps you will learn that certain members weren’t as top talent as you thought, or perhaps the goal wasn’t as clear at it seemed.  The members might learn what behaviors got in the way of success.Regardless of the cause, make sure that you do a “post mortem” review with all of the team members.It will be tempting to focus on what went wrong, but do your best to make the final meeting of the team a brainstorming session to think of ways to make the team successful on the next project.  That way the members will look forward to the next opportunity with new ideas.
There it is - the 5 requirements to make a group into a team.  Transforming your groups into teams with increase their productivity and the quality of their effort.  That teamwork can boost your company from “middle of the pack” to leaders that other companies seek to mimic.
At MSI, we have a talented group who can help you and your group to form a team.  We are running educational seminars and even team building boot camps.  Make sure you sign up on our mailing list and we will tell you about all of our upcoming opportunities!

item.title: Pursuing the Elusive Team
item.description: What is a “team”?   When you hear the word “team” you likely imagine a sports team.  And of course professional sports are excellent examples of highly organized and interdependent teams.  But, what makes them that way and why does it matter in business or research?       The short answer:  A fully functional team is always more efficient, effective and dynamic than any other group.     A quick quiz – identify the team from the following examples: A group of interested people volunteering
item.dc:creator: Dr. David Kiewlich
item.content:encoded:
What is a “team”?   When you hear the word “team” you likely imagine a sports team.  And of course professional sports are excellent examples of highly organized and interdependent teams.  But, what makes them that way and why does it matter in business or research?
The short answer:  A fully functional team is always more efficient, effective and dynamic than any other group.
A quick quiz – identify the team from the following examples:
A group of interested people volunteering together to brainstorm a new project.A group that have worked together for many years and frequently get together after work hours to interact socially.A hierarchal structure with a defined leader and subordinates with established goals working together for the first time.
In fact, it isn’t the first or the second and it may not be the third either.  A team has to meet several criteria to work together.  Simply calling a group a team doesn’t make it so, nor does it necessarily help the process.
So then, what criteria are critical to make a team?
First is assembling the best people.
It sounds simple, but unless you know what talent you need to accomplish the goal, you cannot select the right participants.  Teams usually don’t spontaneously come together with the best individuals.  Know what you need and then go and get it.
Interestingly enough, compatible personalities are not always required.  It helps to have a group that works well together, but ultimately that only reduces the amount of administrative oversight and leadership required.Don’t include someone so disruptive that he/she will require major amounts of external discipline to keep them in line, but, at the same time, don’t go only with people who work well together unless they are your absolute best talent.
In the first example above, the members came together spontaneously; they weren’t selected in any way.  They may be the best the company has, but likely not.  These groups require little management, since they self-select, but that doesn’t mean they are the best people available.
Similarly, in the second example, just because people have worked together for a long time and socialize outside of work hours, doesn’t mean they have the best skills and talent to achieve the goal.  In fact, depending on the professionalism of the individuals, they may be less motivated to work hard because they are “family” and don’t want to step on each other’s toes.
In the third example, if the members were selected with care and a focus on top talent, the fact that they are working together for the first time is not necessarily an issue.
Second, build the team with a very specific set of goals and duties for that team to accomplish.
Even all-star talent will not accomplish anything if they are not clear on what is expected of them.   Oftentimes there are important goals, like delivering a new compound for preclinical analysis or submitting 15 new patents this year, but without a clear path to achieve that goal, members will either get frustrated and distracted, leading to poor morale and unneeded disruption, or they will pursue tangential goals that they view as critical, but may not advance the team toward its stated achievement, also known as mission creep.
Just like in a sports team, each member of a business or research team needs to know why they are part of the group and what role they are expected to play.
Look forward to the next posting with the final three criteria for teams and tips to help you create and manage teams more effectively!

item.title: Why I chose MSI
item.description: Hello, my name is Dr. David Kiewlich and I wanted introduce myself and tell you about MSI and why I am excited to be part of this team.  Hopefully this will encourage you to join us as well!   I have always been an entrepreneur, although I didn’t know what that meant until recently.  I think the best way to describe an entrepreneur would be someone who is never satisfied with what is.  When I think of the world, I don’t envision what is, but what can and should be and how I can make that happen.
